title: Seismic facies changes along a nearly continuous 24° latitudinal transect: the fjords of Chile and the northern Antarctic Peninsula
abstract: High-resolution seismic reflection profiles and piston cores from bays, fjords, and straits of the inland passage of Chile and the northern Antarctic Peninsula region were used to examine changes in seismic facies along a nearly continuous 24° latitudinal transect. These climatic regimes are characterized by differences in seismic facies that reflect the influence of Pleistocene and Holocene glaciation on sediment supply and depositional processes. The study covers Chilean coastal waters from ~41°S to ~65°S, documenting systematic changes in seismic character along this high-latitude latitudinal gradient.

location.description: Chilean fjords and inland passage from ~41°S to ~65°S, along with northern Antarctic Peninsula coastal waters

acquisition.vessel: R/V Polar Duke (Antarctic research vessel, Rice University)

data.name: High-resolution seismic reflection profiles, Chilean fjords and Antarctic Peninsula
data.description: High-resolution seismic profiles and piston cores from Chilean fjords (41°–65°S) and northern Antarctic Peninsula. Documents seismic facies changes along a 24° latitudinal transect from glaciated and fjord environments.

processing.summary: High-resolution seismic reflection profiles acquired with sparker or boomer sources from Chilean fjords (41°–65°S) and northern Antarctic Peninsula processed to produce seismic stratigraphic sections. Standard high-resolution seismic processing applied (demultiplexing, filter, stack). Piston cores collected at key sites to constrain seismic facies interpretation. Facies changes documented across the 24° latitudinal transect and correlated with climate and glaciation history.
processing.notes: 1997 paper; pre-dates most data repositories. Rice University / John B. Anderson group. Covers Chilean fjord system from 41°S to 65°S — unusual coverage of austral Chile. High-resolution sparker/boomer source, not deep-penetration airgun.

analysis_notes: Paper behind paywall; no open access version found. 1997 paper predates standard data repositories. Rice University Anderson research group known for Antarctic and southern Chile work. Vessel likely Polar Duke (NSF/USAP). Data likely archived with NSF/USAP but not in standard marine seismic repositories. Sparker/boomer source — shallow, high-resolution, not deep crustal seismic.
